domain: Reward Model & Verifier Training Platforms for SLM Reasoning
scoring_notes: demand: 0-10 (validated user need), competition: 0-10 (10=no competition, 0=saturated), feasibility: 0-10 (technical buildability in 3-6 months by small team)

ranked_gaps.title: Custom domain verifier training is extremely manual
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A platform with domain verifier templates, guided workflows, and automated reward function generation for domains beyond math/code. Users pick a domain (legal, medical, finance, science), provide example problems with solutions, and the platform generates verifiable reward functions using rubric decomposition (inspired by RaR/K2V approaches). Includes a template library, synthetic data generator for the domain, and evaluation harness.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: ML engineers at enterprises applying LLM reasoning to domain-specific tasks (legal analysis, medical diagnosis, financial modeling). Research labs exploring RLVR in new domains. Startups building vertical AI products that need verified reasoning.
ranked_gaps.why_now: RLVR beyond math/code is THE unsolved problem in 2026. K2V (ICLR 2026), Rubrics-as-Rewards, and Golden Goose (synthesizing RLVR tasks from unverifiable text) all appeared in 2025-2026, proving both the demand and early feasibility. Blog posts explicitly call this 'the verifier problem nobody has solved.' Every team building domain-specific reasoning models is re-inventing this from scratch.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: High. Requires deep understanding of verifier design patterns, LLM-as-judge calibration, rubric decomposition, and domain-specific evaluation. The hard part is making templates generalizable without sacrificing verification quality.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $50M-200M TAM. Every enterprise deploying reasoning models in regulated industries (legal, healthcare, finance) needs domain verifiers. Adjacent to the broader LLM fine-tuning market ($2B+ by 2027).

ranked_gaps.title: No SLM-optimized reward model training toolkit
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A lightweight Python library for training PRMs, ORMs, and verifiers specifically for 1B-7B models on a single consumer GPU (24GB). Built on top of PEFT/QLoRA with optimized memory management. Includes pre-configured recipes for common SLMs (Qwen, Phi, Llama-small, Gemma). One-command training: `verifier train --base qwen-3b --data my_prefs.jsonl --type prm`.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Independent ML researchers, hobbyists on r/LocalLLaMA, startups with limited GPU budgets, university labs. The massive community that trains SLMs on consumer hardware but currently can only use rule-based GRPO rewards because RM training is too resource-intensive.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Post-DeepSeek R1 wave created massive demand for SLM reasoning training. Unsloth's GRPO tutorial is hugely popular but only supports rule-based rewards. TinyZero reproduction went viral on HN. Everyone wants trained verifiers but existing tools (OpenRLHF, veRL) target multi-GPU clusters. The gap between 'I can GRPO-train on my 3090' and 'I can train a PRM on my 3090' is huge.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Core technology exists (TRL + PEFT + bitsandbytes). Main work is packaging, memory optimization for RM-specific architectures, good defaults, and extensive testing across SLM families. Could build MVP in 6-8 weeks.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $15M-50M TAM. Open-source tool with potential for managed cloud offering. Monetization via premium features, cloud training, or enterprise support. Community is large (r/LocalLLaMA has 500K+ members).

ranked_gaps.title: No automated step-labeling pipeline for PRM data
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A production pipeline that takes a domain, sample problems, and a policy model, then automatically generates step-labeled PRM training data. Implements multiple labeling strategies (Math-Shepherd completers, VersaPRM weak/strong consistency, EDU-PRM entropy-driven sampling, ActPRM active learning) behind a unified API. Users configure strategy, run pipeline, get labeled dataset.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Anyone training PRMs. The annotation cost is the #1 bottleneck -- PRM800K required human experts and is still the gold standard years later because generating equivalent quality data is so hard. Research labs, companies building reasoning models, teams adapting PRMs to new domains.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Multiple 2025-2026 papers (ActPRM, EDU-PRM, ThinkPRM, VersaPRM) independently solved pieces of this problem but each is a research prototype. The techniques are proven but not productized. ThinkPRM showed you only need 1% of PRM800K labels with the right approach, making automated pipelines far more viable.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium-High. Must implement multiple labeling strategies, handle diverse domains, manage compute budgets for completer-based approaches. Quality assurance of auto-generated labels is the hardest part.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $20M-80M TAM. Critical infrastructure for PRM training. Could be standalone tool or integrated into larger RM training platform. Data quality tooling commands premium pricing.

ranked_gaps.title: No end-to-end verifier-guided reasoning training loop for SLMs
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: An integrated platform that automates the full loop: (1) generate reasoning traces from policy SLM, (2) auto-label steps, (3) train PRM/verifier, (4) use verifier to guide RL training (GRPO/PPO/REINFORCE++), (5) evaluate on benchmarks, (6) iterate. Single config file, one command to run. Handles data flow between stages automatically.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: ML teams wanting to reproduce DeepSeek R1-style training without stitching 3-5 tools together. Startups building reasoning-capable SLMs. Research labs running ablation studies on the full training pipeline.
ranked_gaps.why_now: DeepLearning.AI launched GRPO courses showing massive demand. Red Hat Developer blog documents the pain of multi-tool orchestration. The individual components exist (TRL, OpenRLHF, data generation papers) but nobody has integrated them. 2026 is when the pieces are mature enough to combine.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: High. Requires orchestrating multiple training stages, managing GPU memory across generation/training phases, handling failures gracefully. Each stage has different compute profiles. Integration testing is extensive.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $30M-100M TAM. Premium developer tool / platform play. Could evolve into managed service. Competes with OpenRLHF/veRL but differentiated by end-to-end integration and SLM focus.

ranked_gaps.title: No tree search + verifier inference toolkit for SLMs
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A production-ready inference library: plug in your SLM + verifier, configure search strategy (MCTS, beam search, best-of-N, weighted voting), get verified reasoning outputs via API. Optimized for latency with batched verification, caching, and early termination. Deployable as a server or embedded library.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams deploying SLMs in production who want to boost accuracy without scaling model size. Companies using SLMs for reasoning tasks (math tutoring, code generation, analytical workflows) where correctness matters more than latency.
ranked_gaps.why_now: LLaMA-Berry achieved 96.1% on GSM8K with MCTS + verifier on small models. Sebastian Raschka endorsed inference-time scaling as the key technique for SLMs. OpenR and sdiehl/prm demonstrate the concept but are research code. No production toolkit exists despite proven 10-30% accuracy gains.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Core algorithms (MCTS, beam search) are well-understood. Main challenges: latency optimization, efficient batching of verification calls, configurable search budgets, integration with common serving frameworks (vLLM, TGI, Ollama).
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $25M-80M TAM. Inference infrastructure for reasoning models. Could monetize as hosted API, on-prem library with enterprise support, or integrate with existing serving platforms.

ranked_gaps.title: No synthetic reasoning trace generator for reward model training
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A tool that generates synthetic reasoning traces for any domain with controlled error injection. Users specify domain, difficulty, error types (arithmetic, logic, factual, procedural), and step counts. Generates both correct and incorrect traces with known labels. Supports multiple generation strategies (direct prompting, perturbation of correct traces, adversarial generation).
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Anyone training reward models who needs diverse training data. Especially valuable for domains where real reasoning traces are scarce. Research labs running RM training experiments. Teams building PRMs for new domains without existing datasets.
ranked_gaps.why_now: SYNTHETIC-1 released 2M reasoning traces from DeepSeek-R1 showing massive interest in synthetic reasoning data. SWiRL (2025) demonstrates iterative synthetic data generation for multi-step reasoning. Golden Goose (2026) shows you can synthesize RLVR tasks from unverifiable text. The techniques are proven but no general-purpose tool exists.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Core is prompt engineering + LLM generation + quality filtering. Controlled error injection is the novel part. Needs good domain adaptation and configurable error taxonomies.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $15M-60M TAM. Data generation tooling for ML training. Could be standalone or part of broader RM training platform. Synthetic data market is growing rapidly.

ranked_gaps.title: No unified PRM+ORM+GenRM training platform
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A single framework supporting PRM, ORM, and GenRM (generative verifier) training with shared data pipelines, evaluation, and comparison tools. Users can train all three verifier types on the same data and compare performance. Includes GenRM fine-tuning (the newest and most underserved approach) alongside standard discriminative RM training.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: ML researchers comparing verification approaches. Teams deciding which verifier type to use for their use case. Anyone wanting to try GenRM/ThinkPRM but lacking tooling (currently requires custom implementation).
ranked_gaps.why_now: GenRM (ICLR 2025) and ThinkPRM showed generative verifiers dramatically outperform discriminative ones (5% to 45.3% on algorithmic tasks, using only 1% of labels). Yet no framework supports GenRM training. The field is moving from 'which RM type is best' to 'use all three and ensemble,' but tooling hasn't caught up.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium-High. PRM and ORM training exist in TRL/OpenRLHF. The hard part is adding GenRM training (fine-tuning LLMs as verifiers with CoT) and building the comparison/evaluation layer across all three types.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $10M-40M TAM. Niche ML tooling market. Value is in research acceleration and helping teams make informed verifier architecture decisions.

ranked_gaps.title: No multi-domain PRM transfer learning toolkit
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A toolkit for adapting pretrained PRMs to new domains. Start from AURORA's Universal-PRM-7B or a math-trained PRM, provide domain-specific examples, and fine-tune a domain-adapted verifier. Implements VersaPRM's synthetic data approach for domain adaptation with minimal user effort.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams that want domain-specific verifiers but don't want to train from scratch. Enterprises applying reasoning models to specialized fields. Researchers studying PRM generalization across domains.
ranked_gaps.why_now: VersaPRM (ICML 2025) proved math PRMs transfer poorly to other domains (only 1.3% gain in Law) but showed synthetic data can bridge the gap. AURORA released Universal-PRM-7B as a starting point. The base models and techniques exist but no user-friendly adaptation tool does.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Builds on existing PRM models and fine-tuning techniques. Main work is the domain adaptation pipeline (synthetic data generation, curriculum, evaluation).
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $10M-40M TAM. Overlaps with GAP-003 (domain verifiers). Could be a feature within a larger platform rather than standalone.

ranked_gaps.title: No visual/interactive tool for PRM data inspection and debugging
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: An interactive web UI for browsing reasoning traces with color-coded step-level rewards, filtering by error type, editing labels, and analyzing verifier accuracy per step type. Think 'Argilla but for PRM data' -- step-level reward overlay, error pattern heatmaps, reward distribution histograms, and data quality metrics.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: ML engineers debugging PRM training data quality. Teams using automated labeling who need to verify output quality. Researchers studying where verifiers fail.
ranked_gaps.why_now: As PRM training becomes more common, data quality debugging becomes critical. Argilla handles preference data but has zero PRM-specific features. The user base is growing as more teams train PRMs.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Low-Medium. Web UI with data visualization. No ML training required. Could build MVP in 4-6 weeks. Main challenge is designing intuitive UX for step-level reward inspection.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $5M-20M TAM. Developer tooling / data quality niche. Could be open-source with enterprise features. Natural add-on to RM training platforms.

ranked_gaps.title: No plug-and-play verifier marketplace/registry
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A specialized registry/hub for pretrained verifiers with standardized metadata: domain, base model compatibility, size, benchmark scores (RewardBench, ProcessBench), deployment format, and one-click deployment guides. Search by domain + model size + task type. Compatibility matrix showing which verifier works with which policy model.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Practitioners who want to use pretrained verifiers without training their own. Teams evaluating which verifier to use. The growing community of SLM deployers who want plug-and-play verification.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Verifier models are proliferating on HuggingFace (Skywork, AURORA, Math-Shepherd, etc.) but discovery and comparison is fragmented. As the ecosystem matures, a curated registry adds increasing value.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Low. Primarily a web application with model metadata, benchmarks, and deployment guides. Could start as a curated list and evolve. Low barrier to MVP.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $5M-15M TAM. Platform/marketplace play. Monetization through hosted deployment, premium benchmarks, or enterprise curation. Faces platform risk from HuggingFace.

ranked_gaps.title: No reward model distillation pipeline (large RM to small RM)
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A distillation pipeline: take a large pretrained RM (70B+), distill into a small verifier (1-3B) optimized for inference-time use alongside a specific SLM. Includes verification-specific loss functions, calibration, and benchmark comparison between teacher and student.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams deploying SLMs with verifiers in production where the verifier must be small enough to run alongside the policy model. Edge deployment scenarios. Cost-sensitive inference pipelines.
ranked_gaps.why_now: Skywork released PRM-1.5B alongside 7B showing demand for small verifiers. Inference-time compute scaling makes small, fast verifiers valuable. But distillation is a general technique -- unclear if RM-specific tooling adds enough value.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Knowledge distillation is well-understood. RM-specific innovations needed: verification-aware loss functions, calibration for step-level rewards, maintaining discrimination ability at smaller scale.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $5M-20M TAM. Niche within model compression market. More likely a feature in a broader toolkit than a standalone product.

ranked_gaps.title: No reward model benchmarking suite for SLM-specific evaluation
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A benchmark suite testing verifier accuracy on SLM-generated reasoning traces across math, code, logic, and domain-specific tasks. Includes SLM-specific failure modes (truncated reasoning, repetition loops, shallow chain-of-thought) that large models don't exhibit. Leaderboard for verifiers evaluated on SLM outputs.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Researchers publishing PRM/verifier papers. Teams selecting verifiers for SLM deployment. Verifier developers wanting standardized evaluation.
ranked_gaps.why_now: RewardBench and ProcessBench exist but don't target SLM-specific patterns. As SLM reasoning training grows, SLM-specific evaluation becomes necessary. ToolPRMBench (2026) shows the trend toward specialized RM benchmarks.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Requires curating SLM-generated traces, defining failure taxonomies, building evaluation harness. No training needed, but dataset quality is critical.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $3M-10M TAM. Research infrastructure. Monetization is indirect (reputation, consulting, data licensing). More impactful as open-source community resource.

ranked_gaps.title: No managed/cloud platform for reward model training
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A managed SaaS platform: upload domain data, select base model, configure verifier type (PRM/ORM/GenRM), click train, get deployable verifier API endpoint. No infrastructure management required. Includes data validation, training monitoring, and one-click deployment.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Enterprises without ML engineering teams who want custom verifiers. Product teams at AI companies who don't want to manage training infrastructure. Companies in regulated industries needing auditable verifier training.
ranked_gaps.why_now: RM training is complex enough that a managed solution has clear value. But the market is early -- most RM trainers are ML engineers comfortable with code. Timing may be 12-18 months early for full SaaS.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: High. Requires building training infrastructure, multi-tenant GPU scheduling, data security, deployment pipelines, and billing. Significant cloud infrastructure investment.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $20M-100M TAM (long-term). Currently early market. High CAC due to education needed. Better as evolution of open-source tool (GAP-002) than standalone SaaS.

ranked_gaps.title: No code reasoning verifier beyond test execution
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: A code verifier training toolkit that evaluates code reasoning quality at the step level -- detecting logical errors, verifying partial solutions, and assessing algorithm design choices without executing code. Uses formal methods and static analysis as verification signals.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: Teams training code reasoning models where test execution is insufficient (e.g., system design, architecture decisions, security analysis). Research labs exploring formal verification for LLM training.
ranked_gaps.why_now: HERMES integrates formal verification for math reasoning. Similar approaches could work for code. But test execution works well enough for most code RLVR scenarios, limiting immediate demand.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Very High. Combining formal methods with ML verification is an unsolved research problem. Static analysis tools exist but integrating them as reward signals for RL training is novel and technically challenging.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $5M-25M TAM. Niche research tool. Demand is speculative -- test execution may remain sufficient for most use cases.

ranked_gaps.title: No reward model version control and experiment tracking
ranked_gaps.what_to_build: An experiment tracking system for RM-policy co-evolution: version reward models alongside policy models, track how RM changes affect downstream reasoning performance, enable systematic ablation studies and A/B testing of verifier strategies.
ranked_gaps.who_needs_it: ML teams running large-scale RM training campaigns. Research labs doing ablation studies. Organizations with multiple RM versions in production.
ranked_gaps.why_now: General MLOps tools (W&B, MLflow) can be adapted for this purpose. The incremental value of RM-specific features is unclear. Market may not justify a standalone tool.
ranked_gaps.build_complexity: Medium. Could be built as W&B/MLflow plugins rather than standalone. RM-specific features (co-evolution tracking, verifier accuracy curves) are straightforward additions.
ranked_gaps.market_size_estimate: $2M-8M TAM. Too niche for standalone product. Better as plugin/integration for existing MLOps platforms.
